forecast.methodLabel: Pseudo-real-time backtest using conservative release lags

methodology.model: Level on intercept, linear trend, and one-month lag
methodology.confirmation: Classical Chow test with within-indicator Holm correction
methodology.robustness: HAC/Newey-West joint Wald test and full-sample CUSUM

headline.narrative: BIC selected a stable single-regime specification; no structural break candidate is reported. This is evidence of parameter instability, not proof that a nearby event caused the change.

warnings: The level series does not reject a unit root at 5%; level and trend breaks may partly reflect persistence.
core.narrative: The latest supported parameter shift is estimated near 2021-11. The adjacent-regime mean was 1.35 units higher; both the Holm-adjusted Chow and HAC tests are below 5%. This is evidence of parameter instability, not proof that a nearby event caused the change.

warnings: The level series does not reject a unit root at 5%; level and trend breaks may partly reflect persistence.
unemployment.narrative: The latest supported parameter shift is estimated near 2022-04. The adjacent-regime mean was 1.33 units lower; both the Holm-adjusted Chow and HAC tests are below 5%. This is evidence of parameter instability, not proof that a nearby event caused the change.

warnings: The level series does not reject a unit root at 5%; level and trend breaks may partly reflect persistence.
opr.narrative: The latest supported parameter shift is estimated near 2022-05. The adjacent-regime mean was 1.05 units higher; both the Holm-adjusted Chow and HAC tests are below 5%. This is evidence of parameter instability, not proof that a nearby event caused the change.

fx.narrative: The latest supported parameter shift is estimated near 2014-09. The adjacent-regime mean was 0.70 units higher; both the Holm-adjusted Chow and HAC tests are below 5%. This is evidence of parameter instability, not proof that a nearby event caused the change.

warnings: A 12-month minimum regime was required because the available history is short; treat break dates as lower-confidence.
The level series does not reject a unit root at 5%; level and trend breaks may partly reflect persistence.
mgs.narrative: BIC selected a stable single-regime specification; no structural break candidate is reported. This is evidence of parameter instability, not proof that a nearby event caused the change.

narratives.performance: The FBM KLCI gained 13.8% over the latest year, with 9.4% annualised volatility and a 6.5% maximum drawdown during that window.
narratives.macro: The index can respond to earnings, global risk appetite, commodity prices, interest rates and the ringgit. These co-movements are context, not evidence that any one macro variable caused the market move.

economicStructure.source: Department of Statistics Malaysia via data.gov.my
economicStructure.frequency: Annual totals aggregated from quarterly observations
economicStructure.measure: GDP at current prices by production sector

years.narrative: Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2015, accounting for 54.7% of nominal GDP.

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2016, accounting for 55.2% of nominal GDP. Construction recorded the fastest current-price increase at +10.3%, while Services added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +46.1 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2017, accounting for 54.5% of nominal GDP. Mining and quarrying recorded the fastest current-price increase at +16.7%, while Services added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +57.4 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2018, accounting for 55.6% of nominal GDP. Mining and quarrying recorded the fastest current-price increase at +11.1%, while Services added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +57.6 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2019, accounting for 56.9% of nominal GDP. Services recorded the fastest current-price increase at +6.8%, while Services added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +55.1 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2020, accounting for 57.6% of nominal GDP. Agriculture recorded the fastest current-price increase at +5.7%, while Agriculture added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +6.3 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2021, accounting for 54.4% of nominal GDP. Mining and quarrying recorded the fastest current-price increase at +29.3%, while Manufacturing added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +46.5 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2022, accounting for 53.4% of nominal GDP. Mining and quarrying recorded the fastest current-price increase at +41.7%, while Services added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +117.6 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2023, accounting for 56.0% of nominal GDP. Construction recorded the fastest current-price increase at +8.3%, while Services added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +63.8 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2024, accounting for 56.2% of nominal GDP. Construction recorded the fastest current-price increase at +18.5%, while Services added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +64.1 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2025, accounting for 57.2% of nominal GDP. Construction recorded the fastest current-price increase at +11.3%, while Services added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +72.1 billion).

economicStructure.note: Sector shares describe where value added is produced. They are not government revenue, company profit or household income. Current-price changes combine real output and price effects.
economicStructure.message: Official quarterly observations validated; only complete calendar years are published

decisionGuide.title: Decision guide for the current Malaysian economy
decisionGuide.summary: Malaysia currently combines 1.9% headline inflation, a 2.75% OPR, 3.0% unemployment and a +13.8% one-year KLCI price return. The useful response is disciplined scenario planning—not a single buy, sell or career instruction.

signals.label: Unemployment
signals.reading: the national unemployment rate is relatively low

signals.label: USD/MYR
signals.reading: ringgit cost of one US dollar

individuals.title: Build liquidity before taking more market risk
individuals.evidence: Headline inflation is 1.9% and core inflation is 1.9%, so cash purchasing power still changes even when inflation is moderate.
actions: Estimate essential monthly commitments and work toward an emergency buffer suited to income stability.
Keep emergency money accessible; compare deposit terms, withdrawal limits and PIDM protection before choosing an account.
Automate a realistic monthly transfer instead of relying on leftover cash.
individuals.watch: Job stability, household-specific expenses and any variable-rate debt matter more than the national average alone.

individuals.title: Use goals and diversification, not the latest index move
individuals.evidence: The FBM KLCI's latest one-year price return is +13.8% and its measured one-year volatility is 9.4%.
actions: Match any equity allocation to the time horizon, loss capacity and need for near-term cash.
Review diversification across companies, sectors and asset types; the KLCI represents large-cap shares, not the whole market.
Verify that intermediaries and products are authorised by the Securities Commission before transferring money.
individuals.watch: Recent performance is not a forecast. Returns shown here exclude dividends, fees and taxes.

individuals.title: Test affordability before relying on property appreciation
individuals.evidence: The OPR is 2.75% and the 10-year MGS yield is 3.65%, both relevant reference points for financing conditions.
actions: Compare the full ownership cost with renting, including maintenance, assessment, insurance, vacancy and transaction costs.
Recalculate repayments under a higher-rate scenario and a temporary income interruption.
Preserve a separate cash reserve after the deposit and purchase costs.
individuals.watch: Property suitability depends on location, financing terms, holding period and personal cash flow—not national inflation alone.

individuals.title: Use the stable labour picture to improve resilience
individuals.evidence: National unemployment is 3.0%; the national unemployment rate is relatively low, but this does not describe every occupation or region.
actions: Track your own essential-cost inflation rather than assuming the headline CPI matches your household basket.
Use stable employment periods to build portable skills, update professional evidence and explore market salary ranges.
Direct pay increases or bonuses toward high-cost debt, emergency savings and long-term goals before lifestyle expansion.
individuals.watch: Sector hiring, contract type and individual employability can diverge sharply from the national unemployment rate.

companies.title: Review debt sensitivity and idle-cash policy
companies.evidence: The OPR is 2.75% while the 10-year MGS yield is 3.65%. Borrowing still carries a meaningful financing cost.
actions: Run base, higher-rate and revenue-shock cash-flow cases before refinancing or expanding debt.
Match cash maturities to payroll, tax and supplier obligations rather than maximising yield alone.
Separate committed facilities from genuinely available liquidity and monitor covenant headroom.
companies.watch: Actual bank pricing depends on credit risk, collateral, tenor and facility structure.

companies.title: Respond to cost pressure at product level
companies.evidence: Headline inflation is 1.9% and core inflation is 1.9% (positive but moderate); category pressures remain uneven.
actions: Track input, wage, freight and energy costs separately rather than applying a blanket CPI uplift.
Review gross margin by product and customer before changing prices or promotions.
Use smaller, explainable adjustments where demand is price-sensitive.
companies.watch: CPI measures consumer prices and is not a direct index of any company's input-cost structure.

companies.title: Manage net currency exposure, not exchange-rate headlines
companies.evidence: The latest monthly USD/MYR observation is RM 4.0375 per US dollar.
actions: Map contracted foreign-currency receipts and payments by date, currency and certainty.
Use natural offsets first and discuss appropriate hedging instruments with regulated banking providers.
Test quotations and margins under adverse exchange-rate scenarios.
companies.watch: The dashboard describes USD/MYR movements; it does not forecast a profitable hedge or currency direction.

companies.title: Link hiring and capital spending to demand evidence
companies.evidence: Unemployment is 3.0% and the KLCI's latest one-year price performance is positive at +13.8%.
actions: Prioritise roles tied to bottlenecks, revenue quality or measurable productivity gains.
Stage capital projects with decision gates instead of treating broad market optimism as demand proof.
Model downside demand, financing and FX assumptions before approving irreversible expenditure.
companies.watch: A stock index and national unemployment rate are broad signals, not company-specific revenue forecasts.

decisionGuide.disclaimer: General educational scenarios only. They do not consider income, liabilities, tax, risk tolerance, business contracts or objectives. They are not personalised financial, investment, property, legal, tax or career advice.

narratives.snapshot: Headline inflation eased to 1.9% in the latest release, while core inflation was 1.9%.
narratives.forecast: The selected ARIMAX model places the three-month central forecast at 1.80%. Prediction intervals, rather than the point estimate alone, should guide interpretation.
narratives.financial: Inflation, policy rates, the ringgit and bond yields move together through several channels, but these descriptive relationships are not causal estimates or trading recommendations.
